column1:
Before performing a liver transplant evaluation, the Sonographer must review the surgical history to determine:

A: the location of all sites of vascular anastomosis
B: if the transplant was full or partial
C: if the transplant has a piggyback or interposition anastomosis
D: more than one of the above
column2:
Answer: D. more than one of the above

Feedback:
Before beginning the exam, the surgical notes should be reviewed to determine if the patient has a full or partial transplant, a
piggyback or interposition anastomosis and note the anastomosis sites for all vasculature.

column1:
The most common cause of hemodialysis graft failure is ________________.

A: acute rejection
B: arterial stenosis
C: anastomosis rupture
D: thrombus formation
column2:
Answer: D. thrombus formation

Feedback:
The most common cause of hemodialysis graft failure is thrombus formation.

column1:
Velocities in a reversed vein graft are usually _________ at the ________ anastomosis because it is the smaller end of the vein.

A: lower, proximal
B: higher, distal
C: higher, proximal
D: lower, distal
column2:
Answer: C. higher, proximal

Feedback:
Velocities in a reversed vein graft are usually higher at the proximal anastomosis because it is the smaller end of the vein.

column1:
Which of the following symptoms would be associated with steal syndrome of a hemodialysis graft?

A: hand pain that is reduced or eliminated during dialysis
B: shoulder swelling
C: hand pain that increases with exercise
D: spontaneous bruising of the neck, arm and hand
column2:
Answer: C. hand pain that increases with exercise

Feedback:
Steal Syndrome most commonly occurs in a radiocephalic graft. The ulnar artery flow will be antegrade and the radial artery
flow will be retrograde. Blood travels from the ulnar artery into the hand and moves through the palmar arch to exit the hand in
the radial artery. Symptoms include pain, polar sensation, paresthesia, Finger/Brachial Index <0.8, cyanotic fingertips, and
diminished radial pulse on palpation. Symptoms can increase with use of the arm (exercise) and during dialysis.

column1:
The most common site for hepatic artery stenosis in a transplant is _______________________________________.

A: at the origin of the native hepatic artery from the celiac axis
B: at the origin of the right and left hepatic arteries
C: at the common hepatic artery bifurcation located just inside the liver near the portal bifurcation
D: at the native hepatic artery anastomosis site located just outside the hilum of the liver
column2:
Answer: D. at the native hepatic artery anastomosis site located just outside the hilum of the liver

Feedback:
The donor hepatic artery is connected to the native hepatic artery just outside the hilum of the donor liver. The most common
site for arterial stenosis is at the anastomosis site. Myointimal hyperplasia can lead to stenosis at the anastomosis site due to
"over-healing" of the connection.

column1:
Which statement is true regarding the image?

A: There is calcification of the vessels in the vasa vasorum in the arterial wall
B: This patient has fibromuscular dysplasia and demonstrates the string of pearls sign on the image.
C: This is a normal CCA image
D: There is a stent in the CCA
column2:
Answer: D. There is a stent in the CCA

Feedback:
A stent can be identified by the repetitive echogenic foci lining the vessel wall. Carotid stents are commonly made of a
substance called nitinol which is a highly reflective metal alloy. The dense strands of nitinol are woven together to form a
"tunnel".

column1:
Patient presents 9 months after endarterectomy with an approximate 40% recurrent stenosis. What is the most likely cause?

A: Lack of Warfarin treatment post op
B: Polycythemia vera
C: Fibromuscular dysplasia
D: Neointimal hyperplasia
column2:
Answer: D. Neointimal hyperplasia

Feedback:
Neointimal hyperplasia is the most common cause of re-stenosis of a vessel that has a history of the endarterectomy
procedure. It can be described as thickening of the vessel wall or over-healing after the procedure. Atherosclerosis can also
cause re-stenosis but takes a much longer time to reoccur in the vessel.

column1:
If both common iliac arteries demonstrate significant stenosis, what type of treatment will most likely be used?

A: fem-fem bypass graft
B: right axillary - femoral bypass graft
C: kissing stents
D: left axillary - femoral bypass graft
column2:
Answer: C. kissing stents

Feedback:
Kissing stents used for stenosis at the origin of the common iliac arteries. Two stents are deployed, one is placed into each iliac
artery. Both stents are inflated with the catheter balloon. The proximal end of the stents are touching in the distal aorta
(kissing).

column1:
A "snuffbox" fistula connects what two vessels?

A: radial artery connects to the cephalic vein in the radial fossa
B: brachial artery connects to the basilic vein at the elbow
C: brachial artery connects to the basilic vein in the upper arm
D: radial artery connects to the basilic vein in the forearm
column2:
Answer: A. radial artery connects to the cephalic vein in the radial fossa

Feedback:
The anatomical snuffbox is also called the radial fossa. It is a triangular depression on the lateral aspect of the dorsum of the
hand. The radial artery, a branch of the radial nerve, and the cephalic vein are found in the snuffbox. Radial artery is connected
to the cephalic vein at distal wrist. Because the artery courses directly over the vein in this location, little vessel movement is
required during surgery.

column1:
Which of the following will decrease with acute renal allograft rejection?

A: echogenicity of the renal cortex
B: diastolic flow in the segmental arteries
C: resistive index from the arcuate arteries
D: kidney size
column2:
Answer: B. diastolic flow in the segmental arteries

Feedback:
Acute renal rejection will result in an enlarged kidney with increased echogenicity of the renal cortex. Doppler evaluation will
demonstrate increased resistance in the parenchymal arteries and increased resistive index. This will decrease the amount of
diastolic flow in the cortical arteries.

column1:
Velocity ratio, volume flow, and peak systolic velocity are measurements that must be reported when evaluating a/an:

A: arteriovenous dialysis graft
B: pseudoaneurysm
C: subclavian steal with vertebral occlusion
D: lower extremity after venous ablation
column2:
Answer: A. arteriovenous dialysis graft

Feedback:
The velocity ratio compares the velocity at a suspected stenosis to the velocity proximal to the stenosis. The volume flow
measurement uses a wide Doppler sample volume to assess the amount of flow moving through the graft. The volume flow
measurement is specific to hemodialysis grafts. Peak and end diastolic flow velocities are also used to assess the graft.

column1: This patient has a brachiocephalic fistula. Use your mouse to position the cursor and click to select the portion of the cephalic vein that is filled with color.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
A brachiocephalic fistula connects the cephalic vein to the brachial artery at the antecubital fossa. The most anterior vessel on
the image with turbulent flow is the cephalic vein. The vessel demonstrating flow toward the wrist (blue) is the brachial artery.
The radial artery is demonstrated with flow moving toward the antecubital fossa (red), which is abnormal. This patient has steal
syndrome.

column1:
A patient presents with a radiocephalic hemodialysis fistula in the left arm. Doppler evaluation of which of the following vessels will be used to rule out steal syndrome?

A: Distal inflow artery, within 2cm below the fistula
B: Proximal inflow artery, within 2cm above the fistula
C: Cephalic vein at the shoulder
D: Cephalic vein at the wrist
column2:
Answer: A. Distal inflow artery, within 2cm below the fistula

Feedback:
Doppler evaluation of the inflow artery < 2cm distal to the fistula is used to diagnose steal syndrome. If flow is moving
retrograde in the distal radial (inflow) artery, then flow is moving toward the hand in the ulnar artery , through the palmar arch
and then toward the upper arm in the radial artery . Normal flow in the radial and ulnar arteries should move toward the hand
and into the palmar arch.

column1:
If a patient had surgery to correct an occlusion of the right external iliac artery, what type of graft could be used?

A: left femoral artery to the left popliteal artery
B: right femoral artery to the right popliteal artery
C: left femoral artery to the right femoral artery
D: right femoral artery to the left femoral artery
column2:
Answer: C. left femoral artery to the right femoral artery

Feedback:
Arterial flow is shunted from the left femoral artery to the right femoral artery (distal to the obstruction).

column1:
An in-situ vein graft exam must include the evaluation of any patent branches because:

A: of increased risk for the formation of an AV malformation
B: of the potential for AV fistula formation
C: of the increased risk of stenosis in the branch
D: they are necessary for proper graft function
column2:
Answer: B. of the potential for AV fistula formation

Feedback:
In-situ and reversed vein grafts should have all branches ligated before being placed as a bypass conduit. This process is more
effectively performed on the reversed vein graft because it is removed from the leg before it is placed as a graft. An AV fistula is
the most common complication of an in-situ vein graft because the venous branches can still be visibly patent. The arterial flow
entering the venous branches can cause an AV fistula. An AV malformation is a congenital connection between an artery and
vein.

column1:
Which of the following is a sonographic characteristic of a normal renal transplant?

A: decreased parenchymal resistance, RI < 0.70
B: decreased differentiation between renal parenchyma and sinus
C: increased parenchymal resistance, RI >0.70
D: prominent renal pyramids
column2:
Answer: A. decreased parenchymal resistance, RI < 0.70

Feedback:
If the allograft is in rejection, function is significantly decreased. The parenchyma resists the incoming flow due to its inability to
filter the blood. This causes increased parenchymal resistance with RI >0.70. 2D findings of renal allograft rejection include
prominent renal pyramids and decreased differentiation between renal parenchyma and sinus.

column1:
If a patient had surgery to correct an occlusion of the right external iliac vein, which of the following pathways could be used?

A: redirect flow from the right femoral vein to the left femoral vein
B: redirect flow from the right femoral vein to the left popliteal vein
C: redirect flow from the left femoral vein to the right femoral vein
D: redirect flow from the right common iliac vein to the right femoral vein
column2:
Answer: A. redirect flow from the right femoral vein to the left femoral vein

Feedback:
Blood is moving cephalad in the venous system. If the right EIV is blocked, flow can be bypassed from the right femoral vein to
the left femoral vein.

column1:
You are performing an annual carotid duplex on a patient that had a carotid endarterectomy 6 years ago. There is a new layer of homogeneous material lining the ICA and bulb. The Doppler evaluation demonstrates turbulence and elevated velocities. Which of the following is the most likely cause for these findings?

A: neointimal hyperplasia
B: progression of atherosclerotic disease
C: intimal flap and dissection
D: residual blockage from suboptimal surgical techniques
column2:
Answer: B. progression of atherosclerotic disease

Feedback:
Within the first month post-op, restenosis is attributed to suboptimal surgical techniques. Narrowing over the first 24 months
after CEA is attributed to neointimal hyperplasia. After 2 years, stenosis occurs due to progression of atherosclerotic disease.

column1:
Color Doppler evaluation of an aortic endograft:

A: is the most accurate method for diagnosis of an endoleak
B: should be performed with increased wall filter and velocity scale settings with reduced color priority
C: cannot be performed on patients who are less than 3 months post-op due to the risk of cavitation and graft migration
D: can be limited by twinkle artifact and color flash artifact
column2:
Answer: D. can be limited by twinkle artifact and color flash artifact

Feedback:
Calcification in the aortic wall can cause twinkle artifact on color Doppler evaluation. Because color velocity and filter settings
are set to lower levels to detect the lower velocity flow in the aorta, color flash artifact is common with patient movement and
respiration. PW Doppler is necessary to confirm the diagnosis of a suspected endoleak seen on color Doppler. Follow up exams
for an aortic endograft are usually performed at 1-month post-op, then at 3, 6, 9, 12 and 18 months, followed by annual
assessment after the first year.

column1:
Which of the following statements describes the findings on the image of the cephalic vein in a patient with a brachiocephalic graft?

A: There is a stenosis proximal to the graft
B: The patient probably has CHF
C: The measurement indicates normal volume flow rate.
D: There is a stenosis distal to the graft
column2:
Answer: B. The patient probably has CHF

Feedback:
A volume flow rate over 1200ml/min indicates possible CHF. A high flow rate can indicate volume overload in the venous
system.

column1:
Which of the following would be considered abnormal in the Doppler evaluation of a renal transplant?

A: Diastolic flow reversal within the parenchymal arteries
B: A resistive index of 0.65 for the arcuate arteries
C: Mildly turbulent flow at the arterial anastomosis site
D: Phasic flow in the renal vein
column2:
Answer: A. Diastolic flow reversal within the parenchymal arteries

Feedback:
Phasic flow in the renal vein, mildly turbulent flow at the arterial anastomosis site and a resistive index of 0.65 for the arcuate
arteries are normal findings in a renal allograft. Loss of diastolic flow or diastolic flow reversal indicates a significant increase in
the resistance of the renal vascular beds which is consistent with rejection.

column1:
A patient presents for pre-operative arterial mapping for the TRAM flap breast reconstruction procedure. What vessel(s) should be evaluated?

A: epigastric arteries and radial artery
B: radial artery
C: epigastric and internal mammary arteries
D: internal mammary artery and radial artery
column2:
Answer: C. epigastric and internal mammary arteries

Feedback:
Internal Mammary Artery: AKA internal thoracic artery originates from subclavian artery; Used in breast reconstruction and as
coronary graft for left anterior descending (LAD) artery
Epigastric Arteries: Superior EA originates from internal mammary artery and shares anastomoses with the inferior EA; Inferior
EA originates from the external iliac artery; Both vessels supply the rectus abdominis muscle used in TRAM Flap procedure for
breast reconstruction; Evaluated to locate the section of muscle with the best perfusion for surgery

column1:
When evaluating a patient for placement of an AV fistula for dialysis, what is the minimum acceptable diameter for the native vein where the graft will be connected?

A: 3.5mm
B: 2.5mm
C: 4.5mm
D: 5mm
column2:
Answer: B. 2.5mm

Feedback:
Hemodialysis requires a native vein >2.5mm for AVF; native artery >2mm. A native vein >4mm is required for a synthetic graft.

column1:
You are performing a carotid duplex on a patient that had a carotid endarterectomy 2 weeks ago. There is homogeneous material lining the ICA and bulb and Doppler demonstrates turbulence and elevated velocities. Which of the following is the most likely cause for these findings?

A: intimal flap and dissection
B: residual blockage from suboptimal surgical techniques
C: progression of atherosclerotic disease
D: neointimal hyperplasia
column2:
Answer: B. residual blockage from suboptimal surgical techniques

Feedback:
Within the first month post-op, restenosis is attributed to suboptimal surgical techniques. Narrowing over the first 24 months
after CEA is attributed to neointimal hyperplasia. After 2 years, stenosis occurs due to progression of atherosclerotic disease.

column1:
A patient presents for a follow up of stent placement in the SMA due to stenosis. How will you determine if the procedure was a success?

A: peak velocities should be reduced compared to pre-procedure levels, but will still appear elevated when compared to normal velocities of non-stenotic arterial flow
B: at least a 70% reduction in velocity compared to pre-stented velocities
C: peak velocities should have returned to normal levels related to non-stenotic arterial flow
D: at least a 50% reduction in velocity compared to pre-stented velocities
column2:
Answer: A. peak velocities should be reduced compared to pre-procedure levels, but will still appear elevated when compared to normal velocities of non-stenotic arterial flow

Feedback:
When a stent is used to treat an arterial obstruction it helps to maintain a patent lumen, but will result in elevated velocities
compared to expected normal non-stenotic velocities. Follow up exams should demonstrate reasonable velocity reduction
compared to pre-stented levels, but normal velocities should not be expected.

column1:
Which of the following is not an expected complication in a 3-week post-op liver transplant patient?

A: biloma
B: portal vein thrombosis
C: hepatic vein thrombosis
D: hepatic artery atherosclerosis
column2:
Answer: D. hepatic artery atherosclerosis

Feedback:
Atherosclerosis formation is a chronic process. Thrombosis and biloma formation are acute complications that can be seen with
a recent liver transplant.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes steal syndrome in a patient with a Brescia-Cimino graft?

A: it is caused by stenosis in the outflow vein
B: a water hammer pulse will be detected
C: pain and pallor in the hand that increases with exercise
D: triphasic flow will be identified in the inflow artery
column2:
Answer: C. pain and pallor in the hand that increases with exercise

Feedback:
Steal syndrome with an AVF causes blood to travel from the ulnar artery into the hand and move through the palmar arch to
exit the hand in the radial artery. It is caused by high volume flow in most cases, but can also occur with inflow stenosis. Both
cause pressure changes that lead to blood flow reversal in the radial artery. The arterial flow is diverted from the fingers and
back to the outflow vein. Symptoms include pain, pallor, polar sensation, paresthesia, Finger/Brachial Index <0.7, cyanotic
fingertips and diminished radial pulse on palpation. Arm exercise can exacerbate the symptoms.

column1:
A patient presents for Doppler evaluation after revascularization of the SMA 1 week ago. The study prior to treatment demonstrated a peak systolic velocity of 4.2m/s in the SMA. On today's exam, the velocity in the proximal SMA has reduced to 3.3m/s. How will you report these findings?

A: The SMA demonstrates normal velocities after the procedure.
B: Unable to summarize the results until pre-prandial and post-prandial evaluations are performed.
C: Unable to summarize the results with the end diastolic velocity.
D: The SMA flow velocity has decreased, but has not returned to normal levels.
column2:
Answer: D. The SMA flow velocity has decreased, but has not returned to normal levels.

Feedback:
The normal velocity in the SMA is <2.75m/s. The SMA flow velocity has decreased, but has not returned to normal levels.

column1:
Which of the following correctly explains why a surgeon would perform an intravascular ultrasound immediately after stent placement in small artery?

A: To assess the velocity of flow in the artery
B: To evaluate the stent position
C: To assess the velocity of flow in the stent
D: To confirm that all plaque was removed from the artery
column2:
Answer: B. To evaluate the stent position

Feedback:
Intravascular ultrasound uses a 10-30MHz transducer. IVUS allows for 360-degree imaging of the artery lumen. It is used to
locate the narrowest segment that would benefit from stent placement, used after angiography to evaluate proper stent
placement, used to evaluate coronary artery disease from within the coronary arteries. The transducers allow 2D imaging only,
NO Doppler capabilities

column1:
Where is the most common location for the surgeon to place a renal allograft?

A: right iliac fossa
B: 2-4cm below the umbilicus
C: left iliac fossa
D: 2-4cm above the urinary bladder
column2:
Answer: A. right iliac fossa

Feedback:
The right iliac fossa is the most common site for placement of a renal allograft.

column1:
What is the most common complication of an in-situ vein graft?

A: infection
B: thrombosis
C: AV fistula
D: rupture
column2:
Answer: C. AV fistula

Feedback:
An AV fistula is the most common complication of an in-situ vein graft. Because the vein is left in its normal position and
attached to the arterial system at each end, some side tributaries persist as residual arteriovenous fistulae. When the vein is
connected to the arterial system, flow moves through the vein and can enter the smaller tributary veins causing an AV fistula.

column1:
When continuous TCD monitoring is used to assist with a carotid endarterectomy, when is the TCD device first activated to begin monitoring the patient?

A: immediately after the endarterectomy was performed but before the wound closure is performed
B: just before the surgery starts
C: within 1-2 hours after the endarterectomy was performed
D: within 24hrs after the endarterectomy was performed
column2:
Answer: B. just before the surgery starts

Feedback:
The patient's intracranial blood flow is monitored closely to identify and count any embolic signatures that may occur during the
endarterectomy, and closing phase of the surgery, as well as in the recovery room.

column1:
A patient presents for evaluation of their hemodialysis graft. The proximal anastomosis demonstrates a velocity of 130cm/s. The mid graft velocity is 275cm/s and the distal anastomosis velocity is 50cm/s. What is your conclusion about the findings on the exam?

A: There is stenosis of the proximal anastomosis and the mid graft segment.
B: The Doppler evaluation is normal.
C: The venous outflow is most likely thrombosed.
D: There is stenosis of the mid graft segment.
column2:
Answer: D. There is stenosis of the mid graft segment.

Feedback:
Flow velocities that double between segments indicate graft stenosis. The velocity increases by more than 2X at the mid
segment. The velocity at the proximal anastomosis is normal as flow within the grafts is usually high velocity. The distal graft
velocity drops significantly distal to the stenosis. This also indicates a significant stenosis is present in the graft.

column1:
Which of the following statements regarding the radial artery is true?

A: It supplies the majority of blood to the hand.
B: Requires normal results from Tinel test prior to harvesting.
C: It can be an alternative coronary bypass graft when the great saphenous vein is not an option.
D: It is the upper extremity artery most commonly affected by atherosclerosis.
column2:
Answer: C. It can be an alternative coronary bypass graft when the great saphenous vein is not an option.

Feedback:
The radial artery is commonly used for CABG surgery. The ulnar artery supplies the majority of blood to the hand. The Tinel test
evaluates suspected carpal tunnel syndrome.

column1:
Auscultation and palpation should be performed by the Sonographer as part of what type of vascular ultrasound exam?

A: lower extremity segmental pressure examination
B: upper extremity duplex for thoracic outlet syndrome
C: lower extremity duplex for popliteal entrapment
D: hemodialysis graft evaluation
column2:
Answer: D. hemodialysis graft evaluation

Feedback:
Auscultation of a hemodialysis graft is performed to assess patency. Normally turbulent flow will be detected if the graft is
patent. A thumping sound can be heard with occlusion. The graft is palpated to assess for the expected thrill over a patent graft.
The distal forearm arteries are assessed to confirm blood is reaching the distal arm/hand. NEVER take the blood pressure in an
arm with a hemodialysis graft because compressing the graft can cause damage.
Auscultation is not typically part of an ultrasound evaluation in other areas. Palpation may assist the Sonographer in locating a
vessel for the other exams listed, but the US system is typically better at locating vessels than palpation.

column1:
The distal anastomosis of a fem- fem graft is demonstrated. Which of the following statements correctly describe the findings?

A: the native CFA demonstrates retrograde flow which indicates graft failure
B: there is a stenosis of the profunda femoral artery
C: there is significant turbulence noted at the anastomosis site
D: the native CFA demonstrates retrograde flow which is a normal finding
column2:
Answer: D. the native CFA demonstrates retrograde flow which is a normal finding

Feedback:
The distal graft is connected to the native artery below the obstruction. As blood flows through the graft to the native artery,
most of the blood will proceed more distally in the leg. Some blood will flow back up the native artery to the level of the
obstruction because the pressure distal to the obstruction is very low. There is a stenosis of the femoral artery present on the
image. There is no significant turbulence present at the anastomosis. Note the very low color scale and aliasing is only present
at the femoral artery stenosis,

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes myointimal hyperplasia in an arterial bypass graft?

A: Not identified on ultrasound until greater than 3 years post-op
B: Caused by the healing process at the anastomosis sites
C: Easily differentiated from atheroma formation
D: Most commonly occurs in an in-situ vein graft at sites of valve removal
column2:
Answer: B. Caused by the healing process at the anastomosis sites

Feedback:
Myointimal hyperplasia is a common cause of stenosis at the anastomosis sites of a graft. Native tissues proliferate during the
healing process after graft placement. It can be identified anytime from 1 month to 2 years post-op. Difficult to differentiate
from soft atheroma formation.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the Doppler tracing obtained from a hemodialysis graft?

A: There is significant stenosis present at the venous anastomosis
B: Stenosis at the proximal anastomosis of the graft is suspected
C: There is reduced volume of flow associated with graft failure
D: The flow pattern is normal
column2:
Answer: D. The flow pattern is normal

Feedback:
Turbulent flow in the fistula is an expected finding. Velocities below 400 cm/s are considered normal. The arterial inflow is
normally low resistance and very turbulent. The outflow vein will demonstrate increased pulsatility and turbulence.

column1:
A patient presents for an evaluation of a lower extremity synthetic bypass graft for suspected stenosis. What area of the graft should be evaluated first because it is the most common location of graft stenosis?

A: in the proximal graft
B: at the anastomosis sites
C: in the distal graft
D: native artery
column2:
Answer: B. at the anastomosis sites

Feedback:
Thrombosis is the most common cause of acute stenosis or occlusion of a graft. Myointimal hyperplasia is the most common
cause of chronic stenosis/occlusion of a graft which most commonly occurs due to over-healing of the tissue at the anastomosis
sites. The rough surface of the connection and the confluence of the vessel/graft cause increased turbulence of the blood flow.
These factors cause an increased incidence of stenosis/thrombosis.

column1:
Which of the following patients would benefit from intraoperative TCD monitoring of the left MCA?

A: left IJV thrombosis
B: right ICA stent placement
C: right carotid endarterectomy
D: left carotid endarterectomy
column2:
Answer: D. left carotid endarterectomy

Feedback:
Flow from the left ICA enters the left MCA. If a piece of plaque breaks off during the endarterectomy and escapes into the ICA, it
can cause stroke symptoms. Monitoring is used to detect emboli.

column1:
Which of the following is a common occurrence after an endarterectomy?

A: Neointimal hyperplasia
B: Congestive heart failure
C: Intimal dissection
D: Intracranial hemorrhage
column2:
Answer: A. Neointimal hyperplasia

Feedback:
Neointimal hyperplasia refers to the overgrowth of normal tissue in response to the endarterectomy. This is a common cause of
re-stenosis of a vessel that previously underwent endarterectomy.

column1:
A fem - fem graft is used to treat:

A: bilateral popliteal stenosis
B: bilateral iliac stenosis
C: unilateral iliac stenosis
D: unilateral femoral stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. unilateral iliac stenosis

Feedback:
A fem - fem graft can be used to correct unilateral iliac stenosis. Blood is passed from the patent femoral artery through the
graft to the contralateral femoral artery at a point distal to the stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following is an abnormal finding for a hemodialysis graft?

A: volume flow rate 500 - 1000ml/min
B: turbulent flow at the anastomosis site
C: high velocity, pulsatile flow in the outflow vein
D: inflow artery demonstrates a triphasic waveform
column2:
Answer: D. inflow artery demonstrates a triphasic waveform

Feedback:
Normal flow velocity within the graft should be >100cm/s. Graft stenosis suspected with velocities greater than 400cm/s.
Abnormal flow volume <500ml/min. If the flow velocity doubles (or more) between two points in the graft, significant stenosis is
suspected. If the inflow artery demonstrates a triphasic waveform, graft occlusion is suspected. Turbulent flow is normal at the
anastomosis site and the venous outflow will also be turbulent and pulsatile.

column1:
A fem - tib graft is used to treat:

A: bilateral iliac stenosis
B: bilateral popliteal stenosis
C: unilateral distal femoral stenosis
D: unilateral iliac stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. unilateral distal femoral stenosis

Feedback:
A fem - tib graft can be used to correct unilateral distal femoral or popliteal stenosis. The femoral artery is connected by a graft
to the anterior tibial artery or tibioperoneal trunk distal to the obstruction.

column1:
Which of the following requires a 2D and Doppler evaluation for a complete exam?

A: Renal or liver transplant
B: Thoracic outlet syndrome
C: Raynaud syndrome
D: Popliteal entrapment
column2:
Answer: A. Renal or liver transplant

Feedback:
A renal transplant requires a 2D evaluation to assess renal size and cortical echogenicity related to the renal sinus. Doppler is
used to evaluate arterial and venous flow. A liver transplant requires a 2D evaluation to assess liver echogenicity and
echotexture. The other abnormalities listed would easily be evaluated with PPG or digit pressures and imaging would not
necessarily be required.

column1:
You are performing an ultrasound on a fem-pop PTFE graft in a lower extremity and you suspect a stenosis at the distal anastomosis. How will you calculate the velocity ratio in this patient?

A: Divide the velocity in the native popliteal artery by the velocity in the distal graft.
B: Divide the velocity at the distal anastomosis by the velocity in the proximal anastomosis.
C: Divide the velocity in the native popliteal artery by the velocity in the native femoral artery.
D: Divide the velocity at the distal anastomosis by the velocity in the distal graft.
column2:
Answer: D. Divide the velocity at the distal anastomosis by the velocity in the distal graft.

Feedback:
The velocity ratio is calculated by dividing the velocity at the stenosis by the velocity just proximal to the stenosis (in this case it
is the distal graft that is just proximal to the stenosis).

column1:
An aorto-bifemoral graft is used to treat:

A: unilateral femoral stenosis
B: unilateral iliac stenosis
C: bilateral iliac stenosis
D: bilateral popliteal stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. bilateral iliac stenosis

Feedback:
An aorto-bifemoral graft can be used to correct distal aortic stenosis or bilateral iliac stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following is part of the standard protocol for evaluating a patient with an aortic endograft?

A: Document the location of the proximal attachment site and compare to prior exam to rule out graft migration
B: Obtain waveforms in the mesenteric arteries to document flow is unaffected by the graft
C: Obtain waveforms in the renal arteries and veins to document flow is unaffected by the graft
D: Measure the thickness of the mural thrombus surrounding the graft and compare to the prior exam to rule out increased thrombus formation
column2:
Answer: A. Document the location of the proximal attachment site and compare to prior exam to rule out graft migration

Feedback:
Graft migration can be a complication of an endograft. On each evaluation, the location of the proximal attachment site should
be identified and the level of the attachment in the aorta should be documented. Distal migration can cause kinking, tortuosity
or luminal narrowing of the graft.

column1:
After an endarterectomy or carotid stent placement, the first follow up exam should be performed:

A: immediately following the procedure
B: within one month after the procedure
C: on the 1-year anniversary date of the procedure
D: within 5 days of the procedure
column2:
Answer: B. within one month after the procedure

Feedback:
A patient that has had an endarterectomy or carotid stent placement is usually evaluated within first month following the
procedure. If performed too soon after the endarterectomy or other open surgical procedure, artifacts produced by air in the
patch or graft may inhibit the exam.

column1:
You are evaluating a cephalic vein for bypass graft potential. The vessel diameter is <2mm for its entire length. What should you do next?

A: apply a tourniquet to the forearm and remeasure the cephalic vein segments proximal to the tourniquet
B: apply a tourniquet to the upper arm and remeasure the cephalic vein segments distal to the tourniquet
C: report the vein as acceptable for harvest with a maximum diameter of 2mm
D: report the vein as unusable with a diameter at 2mm and select another upper extremity vein to evaluate for a potential graft
column2:
Answer: B. apply a tourniquet to the upper arm and remeasure the cephalic vein segments distal to the tourniquet

Feedback:
>3mm veins are preferred for graft procedures. The basilic and cephalic veins can be acceptable conduits. When performing the
exam, the patient is supine with arm extended at side at a level lower than the heart or in a seated position with hand on lap.
Tourniquets can be applied to the proximal extremity in cases where the veins are very small. The veins distal to the tourniquet
should dilate to provide an accurate maximum diameter.

column1:
Which of the following describes the expected changes in the extremity flow patterns if balloon angioplasty of a 75% stenosis in the common femoral artery was a success?

A: monophasic flow in the femoral artery is now triphasic
B: triphasic flow in the femoral artery is now biphasic
C: increased diastolic flow in the femoral artery and all arteries distally
D: triphasic flow in the femoral artery is now monophasic
column2:
Answer: A. monophasic flow in the femoral artery is now triphasic

Feedback:
Abnormal flow in the lower extremity is demonstrated as a monophasic waveform with increased diastolic flow. After
treatment, the flow pattern in the distal arteries should increase in resistance to become biphasic or triphasic.

column1:
________________ is the most common endoleak and usually does not require intervention, while _____________ is most dangerous and requires surgical intervention.

A: Type I, Type II
B: Type III, Type IV
C: Type II, Type I
D: Type IV, Type I
column2:
Answer: C. Type II, Type I

Feedback:
Type II is the most common endoleak and usually does not require intervention, while Type I is most dangerous and requires
surgical intervention.

column1:
Which statement best describes the Doppler tracing from a bypass graft in the lower extremity?

A: There is increased velocity and post stenotic turbulence present which indicates significant stenosis is present.
B: The waveform is normal velocity and appearance for the lower extremity graft segment being evaluated.
C: There is normal velocity flow with mild spectral broadening present which indicates no significant disease is present.
D: There is increased velocity and spectral broadening present which indicates significant stenosis is present.
column2:
Answer: D. There is increased velocity and spectral broadening present which indicates significant stenosis is present.

Feedback:
The velocity appears to be approximately 300cm/s which is abnormal. The absence of the spectral window indicates spectral
broadening.

column1:
What native artery is commonly used for the arterial anastomosis in a renal transplant when the external iliac artery is not available?

A: external iliac vein
B: internal iliac artery
C: common iliac artery
D: aorta
column2:
Answer: B. internal iliac artery

Feedback:
The external iliac artery is most commonly used as the arterial anastomosis for the allograft artery. The internal iliac artery is
commonly used for the arterial anastomosis when the external iliac artery is not available.

column1:
How does the imaging protocol differ when evaluating a hemodialysis graft versus a hemodialysis fistula?

A: A graft has one anastomotic site and the fistula has two
B: The outflow vein must be evaluated with a fistula but is not required for graft evaluation
C: A graft has two anastomotic sites and the fistula only has one
D: The outflow vein must be evaluated with a graft but is not required for fistula evaluation
column2:
Answer: C. A graft has two anastomotic sites and the fistula only has one

Feedback:
A hemodialysis graft involves a synthetic graft attached to an artery at one end and a vein at the other end. Both anastomotic
sites must be evaluated in a complete exam. The hemodialysis fistula connects a native artery and vein at a single anastomotic
site. The standard protocol includes evaluation of the native artery proximal to the anastomosis, the anastomotic site(s) and the
outflow vein just distal to the anastomosis.

column1:
If a liver transplant patient has a piggyback anastomosis, how does this affect your evaluation?

A: there will be a single anastomosis site in the IVC that must be evaluated
B: there will be two anastomosis sites in the main portal vein that must be evaluated
C: each hepatic vein is connected to the native IVC separately, so there will be three anastomosis sites in the IVC that must be evaluated
D: the hepatic artery anastomosis will not be able to be visualized because it is located deep within the liver tissue
column2:
Answer: A. there will be a single anastomosis site in the IVC that must be evaluated

Feedback:
Before beginning the exam, the surgical notes should be reviewed to determine if the patient has a full or partial transplant, a
piggyback or interposition anastomosis and note the anastomosis sites for all vasculature.
Piggyback - hepatic confluence of transplant liver attached to native IVC; single anastomosis site
Interposition - donor IVC segment replaces a segment of the native IVC; two anastomosis sites

column1:
A patient presents for a 3-month follow-up on his aortic endograft. The aortic sac has increased in size by 0.8cm since the last ultrasound evaluation performed 2 months ago. No endoleak was detected on ultrasound or CT evaluation. What is the most likely cause for the diameter change?

A: Reactive hyperemia
B: Endotension
C: Graft migration
D: May Thurner syndrome
column2:
Answer: B. Endotension

Feedback:
Endotension- no leak present but there is continued expansion of the aneurysm sac greater than 5 mm; sometimes referred to
as Type V Endoleak

column1:
You are evaluating a liver transplant in a patient that received the organ yesterday. The resistive index (RI) for the hepatic artery is 0.9. Which of the following best describes the findings?

A: The resistive index is abnormally increased and any value greater than 0.6 indicates irreversible rejection
B: The resistive index is abnormally increased, but this can be a normal finding in immediate post-op patients
C: The resistive index indicates normal resistance to flow in the liver parenchyma
D: The resistive index is abnormally increased and should be reported as a critical finding of suspected rejection
column2:
Answer: B. The resistive index is abnormally increased, but this can be a normal finding in immediate post-op patients

Feedback:
In the immediate post-op patient, there can be increased hepatic pressure usually secondary to edema. This can cause the RI
values to be elevated. Serial exams should be performed to monitor the expected increase in diastolic flow and decline in RI
values. Resistive Index (RI) 0.5-0.8 Normal

column1:
Venous flow proximal to an AV fistula becomes ________________ due to the inflow of arterial flow distally

A: phasic
B: high resistance
C: continuous
D: pulsatile
column2:
Answer: D. pulsatile

Feedback:
Venous flow proximal to an AV fistula becomes pulsatile due to the inflow of arterial flow distally. The image below
demonstrates the venous outflow of a fistula (proximal to the fistula). The flow in the cephalic vein is turbulent and pulsatile.

column1:
A patient presents for a stat lower extremity arterial Doppler evaluation following the recent placement of a fem-tib graft in the right leg. The patient complains of significant pain and weakness in the calf muscle with areas of numbness on the anterior aspect of the lower leg. What is the most likely finding on the exam?

A: anterior compartment syndrome
B: DVT
C: phlegmasia alba dolens
D: graft stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. anterior compartment syndrome

Feedback:
Reperfusion of the leg via bypass graft can lead to anterior compartment syndrome. Symptoms include paresthesia, pain in the
calf muscle with foot dorsiflexion, drop foot and calf muscle weakness.

column1: This patient has a brachiocephalic fistula. Use your mouse to position the cursor and click to select the portion of the brachial artery that is filled with color.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
A brachiocephalic fistula connects the cephalic vein to the brachial artery at the antecubital fossa. The most anterior vessel on
the image with turbulent flow is the cephalic vein. The vessel demonstrating flow toward the wrist (blue) is the brachial artery.
The radial artery is demonstrated with flow moving toward the antecubital fossa (red), which is abnormal. This patient has steal
syndrome.

column1:
Which of the following vessels are a common source for a Type II endoleak?

A: inferior mesenteric artery and lumbar arteries
B: celiac axis and superior mesenteric artery
C: common iliac artery and gonadal arteries
D: left gastric artery and right hepatic artery
column2:
Answer: A. inferior mesenteric artery and lumbar arteries

Feedback:
Type II endoleak : leak at the branch vessels; will see retrograde flow into the aneurysm sac and increase in AAA diameter;
inferior mesenteric artery, internal iliac, lumbar arteries and accessory renal arteries are potential sources for the leak

column1:
When performing serial exams on an in situ venous bypass graft, _________________ indicates significant graft failure.

A: conversion of the waveform from triphasic to biphasic
B: a decrease in peak systolic velocity of 30cm/s from the last exam
C: a decrease in ABI of 0.10 from the last exam
D: an increase in end diastolic velocity greater than 30cm/s from the last exam
column2:
Answer: B. a decrease in peak systolic velocity of 30cm/s from the last exam

Feedback:
Serial exams that demonstrate a decrease in the PSV or 30cm/s or more and/or a decrease in the ABI of 0.15 or more indicate
poor performance of the graft on the current exam.

column1:
Retained valve cusps are a complication of:

A: reversed cephalic graft
B: in-situ saphenous graft
C: Brescia-Cimino graft
D: reversed saphenous graft
column2:
Answer: B. in-situ saphenous graft

Feedback:
An in-situ saphenous vein graft is performed by disconnecting the proximal GSV from the common femoral vein to connect to
the arterial system proximal to an obstruction. The GSV is then ligated distal to the obstruction and the distal end of the in situ
graft if attached to the artery just distal to the obstruction. The valves must be removed from the graft or they will prevent
arterial blood flow from moving down the leg. Retained cusps can lead to stenosis or thrombosis in the graft.

column1:
In a patient with a patent brachiobasilic AV graft, the axillary vein will:

A: demonstrate high velocity pulsatile flow
B: demonstrate low velocity continuous flow
C: demonstrate biphasic flow with the majority of antegrade flow occurring during diastole
D: demonstrate a loss of spontaneous and phasic flow and will require distal augmentation to assess patency
column2:
Answer: A. demonstrate high velocity pulsatile flow

Feedback:
The basilic vein connects to the axillary vein in the upper arm/axilla. The arterial inflow into the basilic vein from the AVG will
transmit a pulsatile waveform with high velocities into the axillary vein.

column1:
A patient is undergoing AAA repair in the distal aorta. The physician places an endovascular graft within the aorta. If the graft placement was successful, how should the ankle pressures change post- procedure?

A: no change should be seen
B: varies with type of graft used
C: mild increase
D: mild decrease
column2:
Answer: C. mild increase

Feedback:
The AAA would have caused a reduction in flow moving to the legs. The lower extremities would exhibit decreased resistance in
the vascular beds to maintain flow. This would indicate a mild reduction in ankle pressure. When the graft is placed in the aorta,
flow to the legs increases and pressure and resistance would both increase at the ankles.

column1:
The saphenous vein has already been harvested from both legs for CABG surgery. What other vein could be harvested for an additional bypass graft?

A: internal mammary artery or subclavian vein
B: subclavian vein or cephalic vein
C: cephalic vein or internal mammary vein
D: cephalic or basilic vein
column2:
Answer: D. cephalic or basilic vein

Feedback:
Cephalic and basilic veins can be harvested for use in coronary artery bypass graft surgery. The internal mammary artery can
also be used as a bypass graft but the question asks for veins.

column1:
Which of the following describes the 2D appearance of acute renal allograft rejection on ultrasound?

A: Decreased echogenicity and size
B: Resistive index over 0.8
C: Increased echogenicity and decreased size
D: Increased echogenicity and size
column2:
Answer: D. Increased echogenicity and size

Feedback:
2D findings of acute allograft failure include increased echogenicity of the gland, increased size and loss of corticomedullary
definition. The RI value will be over 0.8 but this is a Doppler value not a 2D characteristic.

column1:
Which intracerebral vessel is evaluated on a TCD evaluation performed during a left carotid endarterectomy?

A: contralateral MCA
B: ipsilateral MCA
C: ipsilateral ACA
D: contralateral PCA
column2:
Answer: B. ipsilateral MCA

Feedback:
The ipsilateral middle cerebral artery is monitored during carotid endarterectomy for the presence of cerebral emboli and to
help determine if a shunt is necessary during the procedure.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes hemodialysis?

A: Contraindications include abdominal wall scarring, hernia, inflammatory bowel disease or diverticulitis
B: A cleansing fluid flows is injected through an intraperitoneal catheter
C: An arteriovenous fistula is created by connecting an artery and a vein, usually in the arm
D: Can be performed by the patient at home
column2:
Answer: C. An arteriovenous fistula is created by connecting an artery and a vein, usually in the arm

Feedback:
Hemodialysis requires an arteriovenous fistula. The AVF is created by connecting an artery and a vein, usually in the arm. The
dialysis machine is connected to the patient using two separate needles. Blood is removed from the body, sent through the
machine, and returned to the body through the AVF. The treatment requires regular visits to a dialysis center.

column1:
What is the primary reason why dialysis grafts are commonly placed at the antecubital fossa or wrist, and not the shoulder?

A: there is an increased risk of graft rupture with the wide range of motion for the normal shoulder
B: there is an increased risk of graft thrombosis with the wide range of motion for the normal shoulder
C: to make it less visible to help with patient privacy related to their dialysis treatment
D: the closer the AV fistula to the heart, the greater the risk of developing heart failure
column2:
Answer: D. the closer the AV fistula to the heart, the greater the risk of developing heart failure

Feedback:
The AV fistula is created in the arm at the furthest point possible where the vessels still meet the minimum size requirements
for the procedure. The closer the AVF to the heart, the greater the risk of developing heart failure from reprocessing of the
arterial blood that is emptied directly into the venous system.

column1:
When evaluating a patient for placement of a hemodialysis graft, what is the minimum acceptable diameter for the native artery where the graft will be connected?

A: 2mm
B: 3mm
C: 4mm
D: 5mm
column2:
Answer: A. 2mm

Feedback:
Hemodialysis requires native vein >2.5mm for AVF; native artery >2mm; native vein >4mm for synthetic graft

column1:
Which of the following describes the importance of TCD monitoring during a carotid endarterectomy?

A: to evaluate the potential complication of vasospasm caused by the development of a subarachnoid hemorrhage during the procedure
B: to evaluate the presence of embolic events during the surgery to predict the risk of postoperative stroke
C: to evaluate the presence of associated atherosclerotic intracranial disease
D: to evaluate the potential complication of the development of a subarachnoid hemorrhage during the procedure
column2:
Answer: B. to evaluate the presence of embolic events during the surgery to predict the risk of postoperative stroke

Feedback:
The exam performed to evaluate the presence of associated atherosclerotic intracranial disease should be done before the
surgery is scheduled. TCD monitoring provides monitoring for emboli and can be used to evaluate collateral formation.

column1:
The type of dialysis graft where the cephalic vein is anastomosed to the radial artery is called?

A: Brescia-Cimino graft
B: straight graft
C: synthetic loop graft
D: TIPS graft
column2:
Answer: A. Brescia-Cimino graft

Feedback:
The cephalic vein is anastomosed to the radial artery in a Brescia-Cimino graft. Straight Synthetic graft connects from the radial
artery at wrist to the median cubital vein. Looped Synthetic graft connects from the brachial artery to the medical cubital vein.
TIPS is a type of shunt used to bypass the liver.

column1:
You are performing a follow up exam on a synthetic fem-pop graft that is 2 weeks post-op. The native arteries and graft appear normal on the 2D and color Doppler evaluation. These same segments are evaluated using PW Doppler and monophasic waveforms with mild diastolic flow throughout the cardiac cycle are demonstrated. Which of the following is the most likely reason for the discrepancy in findings?

A: stenosis distal to the graft
B: mid graft thrombosis
C: reactive hyperemia
D: collateral formation
column2:
Answer: C. reactive hyperemia

Feedback:
Reactive hyperemia is the transient increase in blood flow that occurs after a period of ischemia. It commonly occurs following
the removal of a tourniquet, unclamping an artery during surgery, bypass graft placement or after vessel recanalization caused
by a device or medication. Monophasic flow with increased diastolic flow may still be identified in the first 1-2 months after the
procedure due to reactive hyperemia. The body needs time to adjust to the new flow pattern. The waveform should return to a
triphasic pattern after 2 months post-op.

column1:
Which of the following is a sign of a functioning hemodialysis graft?

A: pain or pallor in the hand
B: absence of a palpable thrill over the graft
C: palpable thrill over the graft
D: presence of a water hammer pulse
column2:
Answer: C. palpable thrill over the graft

Feedback:
An AVG should demonstrate a palpable thrill as blood flow moves through it. Absence of the thrill or a water hammer pulse is
indicative of an occluded graft. If the graft becomes stenosed or occluded, the distal forearm and hand could become ischemic.

column1:
Where is the most common location of stenosis in a hemodialysis graft?

A: Venous anastomosis and outflow vein
B: Inflow artery
C: mid graft, especially if it is a looped graft
D: Arterial anastomosis and proximal graft
column2:
Answer: A. Venous anastomosis and outflow vein

Feedback:
The venous outflow is the most common location of stenosis in an AVG for dialysis. Because the vein wall is thinner and receives
pulsatile arterial flow, the wall is easily damaged. This can lead to repetitive healing and scar tissue formation that causes
blockage at the anastomosis.

column1:
If a liver transplant patient has an interposition anastomosis, how does this affect your evaluation?

A: the hepatic artery anastomosis will not be able to be visualized because it is located deep within the liver tissue
B: each hepatic vein is connected to the native IVC separately, so there will be three anastomosis sites in the IVC that must be evaluated
C: there will be two anastomosis sites in the main portal vein that must be evaluated
D: there will be two anastomosis sites in the IVC that must be evaluated
column2:
Answer: D. there will be two anastomosis sites in the IVC that must be evaluated

Feedback:
Before beginning the exam, the surgical notes should be reviewed to determine if the patient has a full or partial transplant, a
piggyback or interposition anastomosis and note the anastomosis sites for all vasculature.
Piggyback - hepatic confluence of transplant liver attached to native IVC; single anastomosis site
Interposition - donor IVC segment replaces a segment of the native IVC; two anastomosis sites

column1:
__________________ refers to when the carotid artery is lacerated and the atheroma formation is scraped for removal.

A: A valvulotomy procedure
B: An atherectomy procedure
C: An endarterectomy procedure
D: An angioplasty procedure
column2:
Answer: C. An endarterectomy procedure

Feedback:
Atherectomy uses a catheter with a rotating head and suction capabilities to break up the plaque and remove it from the artery.
Angioplasty uses a balloon tipped catheter to compress the atheroma and expand the vessel lumen. An endarterectomy refers
to when the carotid artery is lacerated, and the atheroma formation is scraped for removal. A valvulotomy procedure is used to
remove valves from inside a vein.

column1:
A venous thrombectomy procedure is usually reserved for which type of patient?

A: patients with phlegmasia alba dolens and phlegmasia cerulean dolens
B: patients with an AV malformation
C: patients with superficial thrombophlebitis
D: patients with extensive venous reflux
column2:
Answer: A. patients with phlegmasia alba dolens and phlegmasia cerulean dolens

Feedback:
A venous thrombectomy procedure is usually reserved for patients with phlegmasia alba dolens and phlegmasia cerulean.
dolens.

column1:
What is the name of the vascular device that is placed into the IVC using a catheter?

A: Wall filter
B: Greenfield filter
C: Pacemaker
D: Stent
column2:
Answer: B. Greenfield filter

Feedback:
A Greenfield filter is placed into the IVC to prevent emboli from the legs from reaching the heart and lungs.

column1:
What type of venous graft is demonstrated in the diagram?

A: In vivo
B: In situ
C: Reversed
D: Greenfield
column2:
Answer: C. Reversed

Feedback:
The proximal GSV is connected to the distal SFA and the distal GSV is connected to the proximal SFA. This information in
addition to the long scar on the medial thigh indicates a reversed saphenous graft.

column1:
Volume flow calculations are used to assess hemodialysis graft performance. How is volume flow measured?

A: Doppler US cannot provide the volume flow information and the dialysis machine must be used to assess the volume flow through the graft.
B: Obtain 3-4 Doppler tracings of the inflow artery and trace the waveforms to obtain the volume flow measurements, then average these numbers.
C: Obtain 3-4 Doppler tracings and average the peak velocity, then use the Bernoulli equation to calculate the volume flow.
D: Open the sample volume from wall to wall within a normal segment of the graft and trace the waveform.
column2:
Answer: D. Open the sample volume from wall to wall within a normal segment of the graft and trace the waveform.

Feedback:
Volume flow calculations are used to assess hemodialysis graft performance. Open the sample volume from wall to wall within a
normal segment of the graft and trace the waveform.

column1:
Which of the following lists the correct cuff placement for a segmental pressure exam performed due to suspected Dialysis Access Steal Syndrome?

A: Bilateral upper arm and wrist cuffs, and 2 digit cuffs per hand
B: Upper arm cuff on the unaffected arm, bilateral wrist cuffs
C: Upper arm, wrist and thumb cuff only on the affected arm
D: Upper arm cuff on the unaffected arm, bilateral wrist cuffs, and 2 digit cuffs per hand
column2:
Answer: D. Upper arm cuff on the unaffected arm, bilateral wrist cuffs, and 2 digit cuffs per hand

Feedback:
When a patient has a hemodialysis fistula, extreme care must be taken when considering assessing the blood pressure within
the vessels of the arm. A BP cuff should never be placed over the outflow vein at any position on the arm. If the AVF is
positioned at the antecubital fossa (or more proximal), then the wrist pressure can be obtained on the affected arm. Digital
pressures can also be obtained. The WBI and DBI values can be calculated using the brachial pressure of the unaffected arm.
The image below demonstrates cuff positioning for an exam eligible patient.

column1:
Which of the following conditions would benefit from intravascular ultrasound with repair?

A: renal artery stenosis only
B: cerebral artery aneurysm only
C: AAA and renal artery stenosis
D: AAA only
column2:
Answer: C. AAA and renal artery stenosis

Feedback:
Intravascular ultrasound can be used in the repair of aortic aneurysms or renal stenosis

column1:
Which of the following is considered a normal finding in a patient with a brachiobasilic hemodialysis graft?

A: Increased resistance and velocity with a loss of flow in diastole in the brachial artery proximal and distal to the graft
B: Dilated brachial artery proximal to the graft with low resistance monophasic flow
C: Triphasic flow in the brachial artery proximal to the graft and monophasic flow in the brachial artery distal to the graft
D: Continuous flow in the basilic vein proximal and distal to the graft anastomosis
column2:
Answer: B. Dilated brachial artery proximal to the graft with low resistance monophasic flow

Feedback:
The connection between the artery and the vein will cause the resistance in the brachial artery to decrease and become
monophasic. The basilic vein will exhibit pulsatile, high velocity venous flow in the area of the graft due to the arterial inflow.

column1:
Which of the following is a normal finding when evaluating a hemodialysis graft?

A: Thrombosis
B: Arteriovenous fistula
C: Pseudoaneurysm
D: Aneurysm
column2:
Answer: B. Arteriovenous fistula

Feedback:
A hemodialysis graft is the surgical creation of an AV fistula (connection of an artery and vein). Complications of a hemodialysis
graft include thrombosis of the graft, stenosis/occlusion, aneurysm of the graft, pseudoaneurysm caused by needle puncture
for dialysis, CHF due to increased flow in venous return to heart, and distal ischemia.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes a reversed vein graft?

A: commonly used to correct bilateral iliac stenosis
B: the patient must wait at least 48 hrs after the valvulotomy procedure is performed to complete the connection of the graft connection
C: stenosis at the proximal anastomosis is a common complication
D: there is less scarring with a reversed vein graft procedure than an in-situ graft procedure
column2:
Answer: C. stenosis at the proximal anastomosis is a common complication

Feedback:
The GSV is removed from the leg and replaced in a reversed position. The smaller distal end of the GSV is attached proximal to
the obstruction in the femoral or popliteal vein. For this reason, stenosis of the proximal anastomosis is more common than
other complications. The larger proximal end of the GSV is connected distal to the obstruction. Because the GSV is completely
removed from the leg, there is more scarring than with an in-situ graft where the GSV remains in its natural position. The
reversed vein graft is turned upside down, so the valves stay open due to gravity (no valvulotomy).

column1:
A patient presents for a renal transplant evaluation. Which of the following native vessels will be evaluated during the exam?

A: segmental artery
B: external iliac artery and vein
C: arcuate arteries
D: distal renal artery
column2:
Answer: B. external iliac artery and vein

Feedback:
The question asks for native vessels, the external iliac artery and vein are the only native vessels normally evaluated on the
exam. The other vessels listed are allograft vessels.

column1:
Image A is_____________________________. Image B is ____________________________.

A: in situ vein graft, reversed vein graft
B: venous graft, PTFE graft
C: PTFE graft, venous graft
D: reversed vein graft, in situ vein graft
column2:
Answer: B. venous graft, PTFE graft

Feedback:
Image A is a venous graft. Note the mild thickening walls of the vein graft and nonuniform diameter. The PTFE graft
demonstrates the double linear reflections of the anterior and posterior walls of the graft that represent the double walled
structure of the graft. Uniform graft diameter is also a sign of a normal PTFE.

column1:
Arterial flow proximal to an AV fistula will be ________________.

A: phasic
B: low resistance
C: laminar
D: high resistance
column2:
Answer: B. low resistance

Feedback:
Arterial flow proximal to an AV fistula will be low resistance.

column1:
Saphenous vein mapping is used to assess the vein for a potential bypass conduit. Which of the following characteristics would still allow the surgeon to use the vein?

A: thrombosis
B: lumen <2mm
C: valvular damage
D: chronic obstruction of the ipsilateral deep system
column2:
Answer: C. valvular damage

Feedback:
Damaged valves are not a contraindication for venous harvesting. Thrombosis and a small lumen <2mm are contraindications
for the use of the vein as a graft. If there is chronic obstruction of the deep veins, the saphenous becomes an integral part of the
venous drainage system of the leg and cannot be removed for bypass.

column1:
The TIPS procedure is performed to correct what condition?

A: Atherosclerosis
B: Portal HTN
C: Pulmonary embolism
D: AAA
column2:
Answer: B. Portal HTN

Feedback:
A transjugular intrahepatic porto-systemic shunt is used to bypass the liver and relieve portal HTN. The portal system is directly
connected to the IJV or Hepatic veins.

column1:
A patient presents for evaluation of a hemodialysis graft that was inserted 1 week ago. Which of the following is an expected finding on the exam if the graft has failed?

A: graft thrombosis
B: arterial stenosis
C: graft stenosis
D: myointimal hyperplasia
column2:
Answer: A. graft thrombosis

Feedback:
If the graft was just placed in the arm a week ago, you are looking for an acute cause of failure. The other three choices listed
are chronic causes of graft failure.

column1:
Which of the following techniques is used to monitor flow during a fem-pop bypass procedure?

A: B mode ultrasound
B: pulse volume recording
C: duplex ultrasound
D: segmental pressures
column2:
Answer: C. duplex ultrasound

Feedback:
Segmental pressures and PVR exams require the use of pressure cuffs that cannot be used during the surgery, AFTER the
surgery would be fine. Intraoperative duplex ultrasound can be used during the procedure to assess flow.

column1:
Which of the following vascular complications is treated with a fasciotomy?

A: popliteal entrapment
B: pseudoaneurysm
C: dissection
D: anterior compartment syndrome
column2:
Answer: D. anterior compartment syndrome

Feedback:
Blood leaks into the muscle compartment of the calf causing increased pressure. If the fasciotomy is not performed to relieve
the pressure, damage to the nerves and tissues can occur.

column1:
Which statement is true regarding the image?

A: There is calcification of the vessels in the vasa vasorum in the arterial wall and the patient should have a TCD exam to evaluate the extent of the disease.
B: This patient should have an angiogram to confirm the suspected diagnosis of fibromuscular dysplasia.
C: There is calcification of the vessels in the vasa vasorum in the arterial wall and the patient should have balloon angioplasty.
D: The patient should be evaluated for neointimal hyperplasia.
column2:
Answer: D. The patient should be evaluated for neointimal hyperplasia.

Feedback:
The image shows a stent in the carotid artery. Neointimal hyperplasia refers to the overgrowth of normal tissue in response to
the endarterectomy and stent/graft placement. This is a common cause of re-stenosis of a vessel that previously underwent
interventional treatment.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding Doppler flow evaluation of a rejected renal allograft?

A: Rejected allograft waveforms will demonstrate high resistance flow.
B: Rejected allograft waveforms will demonstrate low resistance flow.
C: Rejected allograft waveforms will demonstrate low RI and PI values.
D: Renal allograft waveforms normally demonstrate arterial flow that varies with respiration.
column2:
Answer: A. Rejected allograft waveforms will demonstrate high resistance flow.

Feedback:
Doppler waveforms from arteries in a rejected allograft will demonstrate high resistance flow. The RI values for the
parenchymal vessels will be elevated above 0.70.

column1:
When evaluating a patient with an aortic endograft, what is the minimum increase in aortic sac size that indicates a possible endoleak?

A: 0.2cm
B: 0.9cm
C: 1.5cm
D: 0.5cm
column2:
Answer: D. 0.5cm

Feedback:
An increase in aortic sac diameter of 0.5cm or greater indicates a possible endoleak.

column1:
Which of the following is not a common type of lower extremity graft placement?

A: femoral artery to the femoral artery
B: popliteal artery to the DPA
C: femoral artery to the popliteal artery
D: femoral artery to the PTA
column2:
Answer: B. popliteal artery to the DPA

Feedback:
A Fem-Fem bypass graft indicates connection between two segments of the same femoral artery or between the right and left
femoral artery. A Fem-Pop bypass graft connects the femoral artery and popliteal artery in the same leg. A Fem-Tib graft
connects the femoral artery and most commonly the tibial artery. A bypass graft could not be connected to the dorsalis pedis
artery because of its size and location.
